text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California with a video on my technique for closing large defects during robotic incisional hernia repair. This was an ETEP case. The defect was about ten centimeters wide. by about twenty centimeters long I'm docked on the patient's left side and so you can see I'm pretty close to the left linea alba and Later on, I'll point out that I'll have trouble seeing the linea alba at some points, and that is important in my consideration of the technique I use.

The surgeon uses a 24-inch OV-Lock 180 suture, employing a technique to manage the suture efficiently by pulling with the left hand and pushing with the needle driver to prevent entanglement. The suture is laid out across the defect without tightening until the entire strand is used, then tightened to gain more suture length for further suturing. The bedside assistant palpates the skin, noting the thickness of the subcutaneous tissue.

The surgeon employs the progressive tensioning concept by laying out sutures along the defect and gradually tightening them to bring the edges of the linea alba closer together. At the caudal end, the edges are tightened as they are close together, while at the wider portion, the suture is laid out before tightening. The surgeon uses a new twenty-four inch suture, taking small bites of the fascia and sac to prevent seroma formation.

The surgeon uses a 24-inch suture to progressively tension the defect, noting that the linea alba is becoming difficult to visualize as the rectus muscle medializes and the linea alba retracts. This technique prevents the formation of a single large seroma by creating multiple smaller seromas.

The surgeon lays out sutures across the widest part of the defect without tightening them, ensuring the linea alba remains visible. As the sutures are progressively tightened, the surgeon uses a technique of pulling with the right hand perpendicular to the abdominal wall while the left hand braces the tissue, effectively gauging tension to avoid breaking the suture or damaging the fascia. The strands become looser as the fascial edges are brought closer together, facilitating the re-approximation of the defect.

The surgeon parks the needle before starting the next strand to avoid confusion during tightening. The pneumoperitoneum pressure is reduced to ten millimeters mercury to facilitate instrument reach. The surgeon re-approximates the cranial portion of the defect, addressing small holes and weak pockets of fascia, stopping at the tapering of the widest part of the linea alba.

The surgeon revisits earlier suture strands, adjusting them as they become looser while moving from caudal to cranial and back to caudal, demonstrating the closure technique for larger defects. The needle is parked to keep track of which strand belongs to which needle, ensuring clarity during the suturing process.

The surgeon manages multiple strands of suture by walking back the initial two sutures and cutting them to prevent entanglement with the third suture while working on the cranial part of the defect. The bites are placed on the posterior rectus sheath edge, sometimes locking them with a few throws. The surgeon assesses the patient's abdominal contour to address visible diastasis and bulge, ensuring adequate mesh overlap of about four centimeters by dissecting and dividing more of the posterior rectus sheath.

So this is my systematic approach to defect closure. Use a progressive tensioning method. Use as many long strands of V-lock as needed. Pull perpendicular to the tissue brace with the left hand. And here I'm going to the bedside to look at the bulge, make sure I'm happy with the appearance of the abdominal wall. And so I'm going to finish and walk this back. Thank you for watching.
